data.title: Dover 2Q Profit Ticks Down; Guidance Boosted on Order Growth
data.description:
By Rob Curran

Dover's second-quarter net income slipped, but the manufacturer boosted its projection for 2025 adjusted earnings on strong order trends.

The Downers Grove, Ill., maker of industrial equipment such as pumps and automation technology posted earnings of $279.1 million, or $2.02 a share, down from $281.8 million or $2.04 a share, a year earlier.

Adjusted earnings came in at $2.44 a share, surpassing the average analyst estimate of $2.39 a share, as per FactSet.

Revenue rose 5% to $2.05 billion.

"Order trends continued to post positive momentum in the quarter, bolstering our confidence in the second half outlook with a majority of our third quarter revenue already in the backlog," said President and Chief Executive Richard Tobin, in a statement.

For 2025, Dover forecast earnings from continuing operations in a range between $8 and $8.20 a share, down slightly from a prior estimate of $8.04 to $8.24 a share. The maker of industrial goods boosted its forecast for adjusted earnings from continuing operations to a range between $9.35 and $9.55 a share from a previous target of $9.20 to $9.40 a share. Dover forecast full-year revenue growth of 4% to 6%.

Write to Rob Curran at rob.curran@dowjones.com

data.title: A&W Food Services Canada Still Sees FY25 Same-Store Sales Flat to Up 3% >AW.T
Mobileye Global rises on annual revenue forecast raise
data.description:
** Self-driving technology firm Mobileye Global's shares rise 6.2% to $17.10 premarket

** MBLY raises its annual revenue forecast to between $1.77 billion and $1.89 billion vs co's prior expectations of $1.69 billion to $1.81 billion

** "Stronger visibility on industry supply-demand alignment since late-April supports our decision to raise the full-year outlook, while we continue to maintain a conservative stance given the broader macro environment" - CEO Amnon Shashua

** As of last close, stock was down ~19% this year

data.title: THORChain to Hold AMA on X on July 25th
data.description:
THORChain will hold an AMA on X on July 25th at 17:00 UTC, providing an overview of forthcoming network updates and featuring guest commentary from Kenton Ralph Toews.

Refer to the official tweet by RUNE:

THORChain
@THORChain

📢GOING LIVE ON FRIDAY 5 PM UTC

We're hosting a Space w/ @familiarcow - it's been a while, but we love having him on to give us all the alpha on the next updates.

Co-hosts - you're boys @KentonC137 & @patriotsounds

See you there!https://t.co/bJ1PW4inEh

Jul 23, 2025

RUNE Info

THORChain is a decentralized liquidity protocol that enables users to easily exchange cryptocurrency tokens over the network without the need for intermediaries. This is achieved by creating a liquidity pool that automatically establishes prices and provides liquidity for trading.

The key feature of THORChain is its ability to facilitate secure, fast, and reliable cross-chain asset operations. This means that users can directly exchange one cryptocurrency for another, even if they exist on different blockchains.

The RUNE token in THORChain is used as collateral for validators in the network and to ensure liquidity in the liquidity pools.

data.title: Catalyst Bancorp Q2 net income falls
data.description:
Overview

  • Catalyst Bancorp Q2 net income declines to $521,000 from $586,000 in Q1

  • Loans grow by $1.5 mln, deposits rise by $1.6 mln in Q2

  • Net interest margin increases to 3.98%, up nine basis points from Q1

Outlook

  • Company does not give any specificguidance

Result Drivers

  • LOAN GROWTH - Modest increase in total loans driven by commercial real estate and multi-family residential sectors

  • DEPOSIT FLUCTUATIONS - Deposit growth attributed to changes in public funds and commercial customer inflows

  • INTEREST MARGIN - Improved net interest margin due to higher loan income and reduced deposit costs

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 7 AM ET: Global Markets Rise as Trade Deal Progress Boosts Investor Optimism | Global ...
data.description:
Global Markets Rise as Trade Deal Progress Boosts Investor Optimism

Global stock markets and U.S. stock futures mostly rose Thursday as momentum in trade deals buoyed investors' sense of optimism.

----

Global Economies Show Resilience Despite Tariff Fears

Large economies in Europe and Asia continued to show resilience in the face of high levels of uncertainty about the future of trade and international relations.

----

ECB to Pause Rates. Why It's Joining the Fed and Powell on Pause.

In June, the ECB lowered its key deposit rate in for an the eighth consecutive quarter-point reduction. President Donald Trump thinks the Fed should follow suit.

----

Trump to Tour Federal Reserve, Ramping Up Pressure Campaign on Powell

In a rare visit, the president will stop by the U.S. central bank, which is undergoing an extensive $2.5 billion renovation he has criticized.

----

Asia-U.S. Trade Deals Pick Up as Tariff Deadline Nears

Excluding a truce with China, four Asian nations have now reached deals, with just over a week before the grace period expires.

----

Beijing's Trade Tactics, Ukraine Stance Cast Pall Over Europe-China Ties

After Trump's election, China and Europe sought warmer ties, but rifts over Russia and trade sparked conflict.

----

Chinese Social-Media Hashtag Signals Growing Frustration With Job Market

The phrase "beauty in the time of economic upturns" has been trending on Chinese sites for months, a nostalgic reference to the 2000s-2010s.

----

Trump's New Trade Standard Takes Shape With 15% Tariff Deal

A deal with Japan and a potential deal with Europe follow months of uncertainty and will likely raise prices while offering a bit of clarity for global trade.

----

South Korea's Economy Rebounds in Second Quarter

The pickup in Asia's fourth-largest economy was largely driven by resilient exports and policy support.

----

Trump Administration Pledges to Stimulate AI Use and Exports

The administration's artificial-intelligence "action plan" aims to boost construction of data centers and encourage exports.

----

Why gold may be in a new paradigm as the yellow metal soars in 2025

Gold may be in a new paradigm after beating a widely followed gauge of the U.S. stock market over the past two decades.

----

Wall Street braces for deluge of Treasury bills, a crucial test of market demand

The Treasury Department is gearing up to deliver its first big plans for borrowing during the current quarter since President Donald Trump signed his sweeping package of tax and spending cuts into law on July 4.

----

U.S. Crude Oil Stockpiles Fall More Than Expected

U.S. crude oil inventories declined by 3.2 million barrels as domestic production and imports fell while exports picked up.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Financial Services Headlines at 7 AM ET: This Private-Equity Giant Is Expanding. Its Stock Is a Buy. | Lloyds ...
data.description:
This Private-Equity Giant Is Expanding. Its Stock Is a Buy.

TPG stands to benefit from major investments in private credit, real estate, and digital infrastructure.

----

Lloyds Banking Group Sticks With Outlook After Profit Beat

The U.K.'s largest mortgage provider reported better-than-expected second-quarter profit on lighter costs and resilient income from interest.

----

Deutsche Bank Shares Buoyed by Earnings Beat

The lender said its client businesses continued to grow revenues and that its second-quarter results were in line with its guidance for the year.

----

BNP Paribas Guides for Faster Profit Growth in Second Half

The group expects revenue and profit growth to accelerate in the latter half of the year, following results that topped market views.

----

Sabadell to Beef Up Shareholder Returns as It Fends off BBVA Takeover

The Catalan lender aims for a return on tangible equity of 16% by 2027, from 15.3% at the end of June this year.

----

Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on Lloyds Banking Group, BNP Paribas, Sabadell and more in the latest Market Talks covering Financial Services.

----

Lawmakers Subpoena JPMorgan and BofA Over IPO of Chinese Battery Giant

A House committee focused on national security had previously urged banks to stop work on CATL's initial public offering.

----

Macquarie Group's Profit Falls

Macquarie Group didn't quantify the decline in first-quarter net profit, and said that its CFO would step down at the end of the year.

----

Goldman and BNY Team Up to Tokenize Money-Market Funds

The banking giants join a growing list of traditional financial firms seeking to bring crypto technology to assets ranging from stocks and investment funds to commodities.

----

Probe of Davos Founder Finds Unauthorized Spending, Inappropriate Behavior

The Davos founder rejected preliminary findings of misconduct, including improper travel expenses and gifts.

----

CME Earnings Surge on Volatility, Increased Trading

CME Group's second-quarter earnings and revenue rose sharply as the derivatives-exchange operator benefited from a surge in trading activity due to market volatility.

----

Will Buffett's Successor Get the Same Free Pass From Investors?

There are some calls the Oracle of Omaha can get away with that others may not so easily.

----

New Entrants Drive Secondary Market to Record First Half

California Public Employees' Retirement System is among the traditional sellers that have become buyers on the market for private-fund stakes.

----

AI Talent Frenzy Is a Mixed Bag for VCs

Venture investors and limited partners have made money as founders get hired away, but some deals aren't that great for investors.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Energy Headlines at 7 AM ET: Oil Rises on U.S.-EU Trade Optimism, U.S. Crude Stock Draw | Private ...
data.description:
Oil Rises on U.S.-EU Trade Optimism, U.S. Crude Stock Draw

Oil prices rose driven by optimism over U.S. trade negotiations and a larger-than-expected draw in crude inventories.

----

Private Equity Gets Bullish on Natural Gas-Fired Power Plants

Deals involving the assets totaled $49 billion during the first six months of the year.

----

TotalEnergies Maintains Buyback Pace Despite Profit Drop, Higher Debt

The energy major said it would keep its $2 billion quarterly buyback even as lower oil-and-gas prices weighed on its earnings.

----

Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on TotalEnergies, Woodside Energy, Petronas Dagangan and more in the latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities.

----

U.S. Crude Oil Stockpiles Fall More Than Expected

U.S. crude oil inventories declined by 3.2 million barrels as domestic production and imports fell while exports picked up.

----

GE Vernova Stock Soared on Earnings Beat. Wall Street Loves the Power Firm.

The power company posted strong second-quarter earnings, and raised full-year financial guidance.

----

Equinor Takes Near $1 Billion Hit From Trump Flip-Flop on Offshore Wind

Norwegian energy giant said stalling of construction of its Empire Wind project off the New York coast and an issue with a lease cost the company $955 million.

Halliburton Sees Softer Oilfield Services Market as Profit, Revenue Slide

Halliburton expects the oilfield services market will be softer than previously thought after logging lower second-quarter profit and revenue.

----

U.K. Government Approves $51 Billion Sizewell C Nuclear Power Plant

The government said it will take an initial 44.9% stake in the 3.2 gigawatt nuclear power station in southeast England, becoming the project's biggest shareholder.

----

How China Curbed Its Oil Addiction-and Blunted a U.S. Pressure Point

The Chinese government is boosting domestic production and the EV industry in the name of national security. There are 14 million chargers nationwide.

----

Galp Gets Offers for Mopane Discovery in Namibia

The company was analyzing the offers and is confident it will have a partnership deal this year.

----

BP Names Albert Manifold Chairman Amid Strategy Shift

The energy giant picked an energy-industry outsider to oversee a strategy revamp aimed at reviving the oil major's fortunes.

----

Why cheaper power looks unlikely as Trump's big budget law reshuffles the U.S. energy landscape

President Donald Trump's One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed into law earlier this month, will likely change the nation's energy landscape - potentially leading to higher power costs just as the race to grow artificial intelligence heats up.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Company Headlines at 7 AM ET: Dow Swings to Loss as Sales Decline Across All Segments | Honeywell ...
data.description:
Dow Swings to Loss as Sales Decline Across All Segments

Dow swung to a loss in the second quarter after sales declined across all operating segments, reflecting in part trade and tariff uncertainties.

----

Honeywell Raises Outlook After Beating Estimates

Honeywell International logged higher-than-expected profit and revenue in the second quarter, prompting the industrial conglomerate to raise its outlook for the year.

----

Nvidia, AMD CEOs Rally Behind President Trump's AI 'Action Plan'

The leaders of America's chip giants cheered the president's artificial intelligence orders, which could help boost the domestic semiconductor industry.

----

McDonald's to Test 'Dirty Sodas' and Flavored Cold Brews

The burger giant is aiming to edge into cold brews, refreshers and other iced drinks that have driven sales for other fast-food chains.

----

Hyundai Motor Flags Larger Tariff Impact After Profit Drops

Earnings at South Korea's largest carmaker fell in the second quarter, weighed by increased marketing costs and the impact of U.S. tariffs.

----

The PC Chip Business May Surprise as Intel Reports Earnings Thursday

The company's stock is up 17% this year, versus the 12% gain for the iShares Semiconductor exchange-traded fund.

----

STMicroelectronics Shares Slump After Tesla Supplier Warns of Uncertainty

The European chip maker expects third-quarter revenue to be down 2.5% on year, but nearly 15% higher from the second quarter.

----

Reckitt Benckiser Shares Jump After Core Unit Beat, Guidance Upgrade

The stock traded 9% higher after the company's core business posted revenue growth ahead of consensus expectations.

----

The West's Insatiable Demand for Missiles Is Boosting U.S. Weapons Makers

Orders for munitions and interceptors of all kinds are up, buoying defense contractors struggling with costly problems in other divisions.

----

IBM Second-Quarter Results Rise on Consulting, Infrastructure Strength

The tech company's investments in artificial intelligence are helping it stay agile as customers continue to shift their spending to keep up with a shifting macroeconomic environment.

----

Google Revenue Soars on AI Boom, and Investors Eye Spending Surge

The search giant is spending tens of billions of dollars on infusing its products with artificial intelligence capabilities.

----

Tesla Profit Falls, Hurt by Plunging EV Sales

The electric-vehicle maker's net income dropped 16% in the second quarter, marking another quarter of steep declines as sales continue to fall.

data.description:

  • First Northwest Q2 net income of $3.7 mln, reversing prior quarter's loss

  • Net interest margin improved to 2.83% in Q2, reflecting better asset yields

  • Company elected not to declare a dividend, focusing on capital management

Outlook

  • Company elects not to declare a dividend for the current quarter

  • First Northwest focused on maintaining a strong balance sheet

  • Company evaluating future dividend decisions based on strategic objectives

Result Drivers

  • NET INTEREST MARGIN - Expansion to 2.83% driven by increased yields on interest-earning assets and decreased rates on liabilities

  • CREDIT LOSS PROVISION - $296,000 recapture of provision for credit losses due to commercial loan recoveries

data.title: Turkey Central Bank Returns to Rate Cuts Path After Unrest Forced Hike
data.description:
By Joshua Kirby

Turkey's central bank moved to lower borrowing costs for the first time in nearly five months, restarting an easing cycle that had been disrupted by a political furor.

The central bank said Thursday it would lower its benchmark rate by three points to 43% from 46%, marking the first time since early March that it has cut rates. Later that month, a prominent political rival to President Recep Tayyip Erdogan was arrested by police on what the opposition described as politically motivated charges. That move and the following protests, which were met with further arrests, spooked investors, leading to a series of swings in the Turkish lira.

The central bank subsequently in April raised its interest rates, reversing a course of rate cuts begun at the end of last year as inflation began to ease from the heights it had reached over the last few years. Rampant price inflation had hamstrung the lira and left the population battling ever-higher costs of living. Annual inflation touched a peak of more than 75% last year, but has cooled to around half that level as of June.

"Recent data indicate that the disinflationary impact of demand conditions has strengthened," the bank said in a statement.

data.title: PRESSR: LSEG launches workspace add-in for Excel and PowerPoint
data.description:
LSEG Data & Analytics has launched a new Workspace Add-in for Microsoft Excel and PowerPoint, now available to Workspace users with a Microsoft 365 licence. This marks a significant milestone in transforming the end-user experience and advancing the LSEG–Microsoft strategic partnership.

The new add-in brings enhanced data discoverability and usability directly into Excel and PowerPoint, allowing users to access LSEG’s trusted data more intuitively. Features include simplified formulas, enriched visualisations through Annotations and LSEG Charting tools, and reusable content via the Asset Library.

Nej D’Jelal, Head of Workspace and Interim Co-head of Workflows at LSEG, said:

“This is a significant milestone. We’re transforming how financial professionals discover and use data directly within Excel and PowerPoint. By enhancing workflows, we’re helping users make faster, smarter, and more confident decisions.”

Built for financial professionals, the add-in enables the creation of dynamic pitch books. Annotations streamline the discovery of news and events by enabling dynamic chart labelling, while rich metadata and custom tagging power seamless search and content sharing across Microsoft 365.

data.title: Pool Corp Sees 2025 EPS $10.80-EPS $11.30 >POOL
Mobileye raises annual revenue forecast on autonomous driving chip demand
data.description:
Self-driving technology firm Mobileye Global raised its forecast for fiscal 2025 revenue on Thursday, anticipating a rise in orders for its autonomous driving chips as customers clear existing inventory.

Shares of the company rose more than 8% in premarket trading.

Automakers have largely resumed placing orders for self-driving hardware after an inventory surplus — caused by COVID-19-related supply concerns several years ago — led to a prolonged slump in demand.

Mobileye now expects annual revenue between $1.77 billion and $1.89 billion, compared with its previous forecast of $1.69 billion to $1.81 billion.

"Stronger visibility on industry supply-demand alignment since late-April supports our decision to raise the full-year outlook, while we continue to maintain a conservative stance given the broader macro environment," said Mobileye CEO Amnon Shashua.

data.title: CenterPoint Energy misses profit estimate on higher costs
data.description:
U.S. electric and gas utility CenterPoint Energy's second-quarter profit was just shy of analysts' estimate on Thursday, weighed down by higher expenses.

The country's electrical grids have been facing an onslaught of extreme weather and ballooning demand from electrification of industries and the technology sector's data center build out amid an artificial intelligence boom, prompting utilities to seek rate increases to upgrade and pay for power infrastructure.

CenterPoint's operations and maintenance costs increased 5.5% to $715 million during the quarter ended June 30.

The company provides electricity and natural gas to more than 7 million customers across Indiana, Louisiana, Minnesota, Mississippi, Ohio and Texas.

It also raised its 10-year capital expenditure plan through 2030 by $500 million to $53 billion to cater to an anticipated surge in power demand from data centers.

"This year alone we have increased our capital investment plan by $5.5 billion, including the $500 million increase," said CEO Jason Wells.

Power demand from U.S. data centers is expected to nearly triple in the next three years and consume as much as 12% of the total electricity produced, according to a study by Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory.

CenterPoint's net income fell about 13% to $198 million.

It posted an adjusted profit per share of 29 cents during the quarter, compared with analysts' average estimate of 30 cents, according to data compiled by LSEG.

data.title: Dow cuts high dividend in half in reaction to 'lower-for-longer' earnings
data.description:
By Tomi Kilgore

Chemical company's dividend yield was the highest in the S&P 500, but remains relatively high after the cut

Shares of Dow Inc. sank in early Thursday trading, after the chemicals and plastics company chopped its dividend payout in half to save money, as the company struggles to deal with a prolonged downturn in earnings.

The company also reported a much wider-than-expected second-quarter loss, and revenue that declined more than expected, amid weakness in all of its business segments, particularly in packaging and specialty plastics.

The stock (DOW) dropped 7.7% in premarket trading. That puts it in danger of suffering the biggest one-day post-earnings selloff in at least five years, based on available FactSet data back to July 2020.

The company said the next dividend payment will be 35 cents a share, down from the latest payment of 70 cents a share. Shareholders of record on Aug. 29 will receive the dividend payment on Sept. 12.

"Today's announcement aims to ensure we maximize long-term shareholder value as we navigate a prolonged industry downturn and the resulting lower-for-longer earnings environment," said Chief Executive Jim Fitterling. "With this adjustment, we are aligning the payout size to provide additional financial flexibility."

As of Wednesday's close, Dow's stock carried a dividend yield of 9.22%, which made it the S&P 500 index's SPX highest-yielding component.

With the new annual dividend rate, and based on Wednesday's stock closing price of $30.37, the dividend yield would drop to 4.61%, which would make it the 30th-highest yielder in the S&P 500, and is still nearly four-times higher than the implied yield for the S&P 500 of 1.22%.

The dividend cut comes after Dow said earlier in July that it would lay off 800 employees, in addition to the 1,500 job cuts announced in January, in response to continued weak demand. At that time, the company also said it would close three plants in Europe due to weakness in the region.

"This quarter the Dow team advanced several aggressive actions in response to the lower-for-longer earnings environment that our industry is facing, amplified by recent trade and tariff uncertainties," Fitterling said.

He added the actions were needed as the company was providing "near-term cash support and earnings growth levers," which he expects to total more than $6 billion by next year.

As Fitterling explained, one of the big problems Dow was facing was oversupply from newer market entrants who are exporting to various regions at "anti-competitive" prices, which he believes requires "additional regulatory action to restore competitive dynamics." Sounds like he's pushing for higher tariffs.

Separately, the company said it swung to a second-quarter net loss of $835 million from net income of $439 million in the same period a year ago, due to restructuring and severance costs. Excluding those nonrecurring costs, the company still reported an adjusted per-share loss of 42 cents, compared with last year's earnings of 68 cents a share. The average analyst estimate compiled by FactSet was for a per-share loss of 17 cents.

Net sales fell 7.4% to $10.10 billion, below the FactSet consensus of $10.24 billion.

Among Dow's business segments, packaging and specialty plastics sales were down 8.9% to $5.03 billion, as volume increased 1% buy local prices sank 10% due to lower polymer pricing.

Industrial intermediates and infrastructure sales declined 5.6% to $2.79 billion, as volume fell 2% and prices were down 5%, and performance materials and coatings sales slipped 5.1% to $2.13 billion, as both volume and prices dropped 3%.

The stock has tumbled 24.3% in 2025 through Wednesday, while the S&P 500 has gained 8.1%.

-Tomi Kilgore
